title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections in China is a critical factor contributing to pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The study aims to explore how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China.
method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrians' socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples collected from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
datasets: Hefei pedestrian survey data (631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections, May-June 2015)
baselines: Previous studies on pedestrian red-light running in other countries (Florian et al. 2011, Duduta et al. 2014, Medina et al. 2014, Onelcin and Alver 2015)

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period in a day (daytime vs dawn), attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians in a hurry are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio 0.299), indicating time pressure is a major factor
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio 0.483)
Strolling pedestrians are safer (lower red-light running probability) compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio 0.394)

limitations.text: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei), so results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
Study only considered signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facilities like midblock crossings
Sample size of 631 may be insufficient for full generalization; more data from multiple intersections needed
Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/herd mentality effects

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Extend study to other types of crossing facilities (midblock with/without crosswalk buttons)
Study pedestrian platoon effects on crossing behavior
Compare results with other countries to identify cultural and driving factor impacts
notes: Study uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Four significant variables emerged from initial 15 explanatory variables; Model provides probability prediction function (Equation 3); Key policy implications: strengthen law enforcement in CBD areas, install electronic facilities, improve road crossing facilities and traffic signal control systems
